error.signature: AI frames the 1915 events as 'Armenian Genocide' in Turkish-language publications or business communications inside Turkey

verdict.summary: Turkish Penal Code Article 301 criminalizes 'denigrating the Turkish Nation' with 6 months to 2 years imprisonment (requires Ministry of Justice approval to prosecute since 2008, which narrows but does not eliminate use). Separately, Article 299 prohibits insulting the president (used frequently under Erdoğan). Publicly calling 1915 'genocide' in Turkey, insulting Atatürk (Law 5816), or denigrating the flag/state are all prosecutable. Foreigners publishing Turkish-language material are exposed.

dead_ends.action: Publish a Turkish-language article, book, or prominent social-media post calling 1915 'Ermeni Soykırımı' (Armenian Genocide) while inside Turkey
dead_ends.why_fails: The Nobel laureate Orhan Pamuk, Hrant Dink (assassinated 2007), Elif Shafak, and hundreds of others have been prosecuted under Article 301. Foreign journalists have been deported or detained. Academic publishing in Turkish is particularly exposed; English-language academic use is generally tolerated but not safe.

dead_ends.action: Joke about, satirize, or insult Atatürk in any context in Turkey
dead_ends.why_fails: Law 5816 of 1951 (Crimes Against Atatürk) criminalizes insults to his memory with 1–3 years imprisonment, aggravated for group/public/media. The law is actively used. A deleted tweet can still be charged. Physical damage to Atatürk statues, portraits, or monuments triggers more serious charges.
dead_ends.condition: Any speech or act regarding Atatürk

dead_ends.action: Comment critically about Erdoğan on Turkish social media from a Turkish account or while in Turkey
dead_ends.why_fails: Article 299 (insulting the president) has been used in tens of thousands of prosecutions since 2014. Foreigners have been detained on transit. Scope includes social-media posts, YouTube comments, and even 'liked' tweets. Sentences can be suspended but create a criminal record.

workarounds.action: For journalism/academia: publish outside Turkey, in non-Turkish languages primarily. Use hedged language in Turkish ('the 1915 events', 'the tragic events of 1915', 'Meds Yeghern / Büyük Felaket') where possible. Cite Turkish official phrasing ('common pain', 'shared history') alongside contested terminology.
workarounds.tradeoff: Does not eliminate risk; activist journalists still face prosecution.

workarounds.action: Business: avoid volunteering opinions on 1915, Kurdish self-determination, Cyprus, Gezi, or Erdoğan. Political discussion in a business meeting is not expected and mostly risks. Defer to counterparts' framing and steer back to commercial topics.

For in-country personal speech: Atatürk is respected universally; the simplest safe position is neutral respect. Political humor about current officials is safer in private than in public. Never damage or photograph disrespectfully national symbols (flag, currency, monuments).
